nodes.display: Cognitive Baseline and Last Known Well
New Hallucination or Delusion with Danger
Unable to Meet Essential Care Needs
Falls or Inability to Walk Safely
Severe Headache or Head Injury with Cognitive Change
Relevant Medical Neurological and Psychiatric History
Orientation to Person Place and Time
Rapid Cognitive Progression over Weeks or Months
Recent Surgery Hospital or Environment Change
Reduced Consciousness or Difficult to Wake
Cognitive Change with Stroke Warning Features
Sudden Confusion over Hours or Days
Severe Infection or Systemic Illness with Confusion

guideline_behavior.rationale: Interview behavior derived from guidelines may change with recommendations.

local_jurisdiction.rationale: Local pathways and administrative rules may change quickly.

safety_critical.rationale: Safety behavior and escalation knowledge require frequent monitoring.

stable_semantic.rationale: Atomic patient-reportable concepts are relatively stable but still require periodic review.

terminology_mapping.rationale: Terminology and interoperability baselines have independent releases.

clinical_guideline.rationale: Daily automated monitoring provides early detection for high-impact guideline changes; full review occurs only when change is detected or the review date is reached.

interoperability_standard.rationale: Versioned interoperability standards and domain datasets are monitored weekly; detected changes trigger mapping and Coverage review without changing clinical rules.

nice_guidance.rationale: NICE guidance is versioned and may be amended; weekly monitoring detects changes without implying weekly clinical re-review.

public_health_guidance.rationale: Public-health pages can change between formal releases; weekly monitoring is appropriate for the research package.

terminology_server.rationale: Terminology servers publish versioned content; monthly baseline monitoring balances stability and acquisition cost.

assertions: Initial assessment includes cognitive, behavioural and psychological symptoms and daily-life impact from the person and, if possible, someone who knows them well.
Reversible causes include delirium, depression, sensory impairment and medicine-related cognitive impairment; a normal brief cognitive score alone does not exclude dementia.
artifacts.title: Dementia: assessment, management and support

assertions: Recent hours-to-days change or fluctuation in cognition, perception, physical function or social behaviour indicates delirium assessment.
A competent practitioner uses an appropriate validated tool and a qualified professional makes the final diagnosis.
artifacts.title: Delirium: prevention, diagnosis and management

assertions: Persistent memory problems affecting daily life warrant clinical assessment and may have treatable causes.
An informant can help describe changes; this package does not diagnose their cause.
artifacts.title: Memory loss (amnesia)

assertions: Sudden confusion requires immediate medical assessment.
Potential contexts include infection, stroke or TIA, low glucose, head injury, medicines, alcohol or drug exposure, carbon monoxide and seizures.
artifacts.title: Sudden confusion (delirium)

assertions: Sudden face or arm weakness, speech difficulty, confusion, vision change, imbalance or severe headache require emergency assessment even if transient.
artifacts.title: Symptoms of a stroke

assertions: Relevant domains include memory, concentration, familiar tasks, language, time or place orientation, mood, movement, behaviour and functional change.
artifacts.title: Symptoms of dementia

assertions: STOM returned active candidates for memory impairment, impaired cognition, acute confusion, delirium, disorientation, word-finding difficulty, hallucinations and dementia.
Finding site and Severity were allowed MRCM attributes for selected focus concepts; MRCM does not determine diagnosis or urgency.
artifacts.title: STOM memory and cognition terminology and MRCM summary
